commentaries.author: Ambrosiaster
commentaries.content: Paul begins with prophecy, which is the first proof that our faith is rational, for believers prophesied when they received the Spirit. This is given in proportion to the recipient, that is, as much as is necessary for the purpose for which it is given. Commentary on Paul’s Epistles.

commentaries.author: Basil the Great
commentaries.content: No one has the capacity to receive all spiritual gifts, but the grace of the Spirit is given proportionately to the faith of each one.

commentaries.author: Diodorus of Tarsus
commentaries.content: “Prophecy” means primarily the explanation of things which are unclear, whether future or past, whether present or hidden. “Prophecy” may also refer to the interpretation of a prophet’s words. .

commentaries.author: Gennadius of Constantinople
commentaries.content: “In proportion to our faith” not only applies to prophecy but may be extended by analogy to all the gifts. .

commentaries.author: John Chrysostom
commentaries.content: Although prophecy is a grace, it does not flow forth freely at random but is given only in proportion to our faith.

commentaries.author: Tertullian of Carthage
commentaries.content: Much more aptly would they have matched the Christian with the elder, and the Jew with the younger son, "according to the analogy of faith"

methodology.step_1: Parse main page with BeautifulSoup for structure
methodology.step_3: Use Firecrawl to get full content from 'Go to Commentary' links
methodology.step_4: Fallback to BeautifulSoup for accordion content if needed
advantages: Records verses even without commentaries for complete tracking
